Entity_Description: Shirley is an unincorporated rural community on the southwest coast of Vancouver Island, in British Columbia's Vancouver Island South region, roughly 40 kilometres west of Victoria along Highway 14 (West Coast Road). Home to about 485 residents, it sits on the north shore of the Strait of Juan de Fuca between Sooke and Jordan River, looking across to Washington State's Olympic Mountains. Shirley is best known for Sheringham Point Lighthouse, a concrete tower built in 1912 that still marks the strait. The area is heavily forested, with scattered rural acreages and ocean-facing homes.

Short_Description: Sheringham Point Lighthouse has guided ships past Shirley's rocky shore on the Strait of Juan de Fuca since 1912, and the community that grew up around it remains a string of forested acreages and ocean-view homes along Highway 14 west of Sooke. Long_Description:
Shirley stretches along the West Coast Road on the wild southwest edge of Vancouver Island, where the forest meets the Strait of Juan de Fuca and the Olympic Mountains of Washington State fill the horizon across the water. It is a small, dispersed community of roughly 485 people, defined less by a townsite than by the ribbon of Highway 14 that connects Sooke, 13 kilometres east, to Jordan River and the beaches beyond. The 1912 Sheringham Point Lighthouse anchors the community, its white concrete tower standing on a rocky headland that {COMPANY_NAME} crews pass on the way to jobs along the coast.

Property here is unlike the city. Instead of dense subdivisions, Shirley is made up of wooded acreages, off-highway lanes, and ocean-facing homes that catch the full force of Pacific weather. Salt-laden air off the strait works constantly at exposed siding, decks, and fixtures, while the long, wet coastal winters keep surfaces damp for months. These are the exact conditions our {BUSINESS_TYPE} plans around when delivering {SERVICE}: {SERVICE_DESCRIPTION}. We factor in the drive time and rural access that come with serving a community strung along kilometres of coastline, so {RESPONSE_TIME} and {ESTIMATE_TYPE} stay realistic even out here.

The setting is a draw for a reason. French Beach Provincial Park sits just west, drawing walkers to its surf-battered shore, and the whole stretch of coast toward Jordan River has become a magnet for people wanting acreage, privacy, and a genuine ocean view. That mix of full-time residents, weekend cabins, and newer custom builds means every {SERVICE} call in Shirley looks a little different, and {COMPANY_NAME} tailors the work to the property rather than forcing a one-size template.

For homeowners between Sooke and the far western communities of the Capital Regional District, having a {BUSINESS_TYPE} that already knows the Highway 14 corridor matters. We keep {SERVICE_LIST} ready for the coastal conditions Shirley throws at a home, we stand behind our work with {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP}, and we treat rural properties with the same priority as any address closer to Victoria. When you need {SERVICE} you can count on in Shirley, reach {COMPANY_NAME} at {PHONE}.
Local_Facts:
- Shirley is an unincorporated rural community in BC's Vancouver Island South region, about 40 km west of Victoria and 13 km west of Sooke along Highway 14 (West Coast Road).
- It sits on the north shore of the Strait of Juan de Fuca, looking across to Washington State's Olympic Mountains.
- Sheringham Point Lighthouse, built in 1912, stands on a rocky headland at Shirley and is a designated heritage lighthouse.
- The community has an approximate population of 485 (GeoNames estimate).
- French Beach Provincial Park lies just west of Shirley along the coast toward Jordan River.
- Local building stock is dominated by forested acreages and ocean-facing rural homes rather than dense subdivisions.
- The region enjoys Canada's mildest climate, yet exposed coastal properties still face constant salt air and wet winters.
